title: SparseMM: Head Sparsity Emerges from Visual Concept Responses in MLLMs
summary: This paper investigates how visual concepts are processed in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). It identifies a small subset of attention heads, termed 'visual heads', that are disproportionately responsible for visual comprehension. The authors find these heads are both sparse (less than 5% of total heads) and universal (emerging across different architectures and attention paradigms). Based on this, they propose SparseMM, a KV-Cache optimization framework to accelerate inference by leveraging this sparsity.

visual_head_identification.approach: A training-free framework using targeted cross-modal response analysis.
visual_head_identification.anchor_task: OCR (Optical Character Recognition) to establish precise text-to-visual input correspondence.
visual_head_identification.process: Tracing activation from generated words back to spatially aligned image patches to measure how specific attention heads mediate visual-text alignment.
visual_head_identification.output: Computation of 'visual scores' to rank attention heads by their visual responsiveness.

optimization_framework.name: SparseMM
optimization_framework.principle: Exploits the sparsity of visual heads to achieve accelerated inference.
optimization_framework.problem_addressed: Computational overhead of maintaining full KV-Caches for complex multimodal inputs (e.g., high-resolution images, video).

key_findings.description: Less than 5% of attention heads are intrinsically visual-active across layers, even in models trained with extensive multimodal data.
Visual heads emerge consistently across diverse LLM architectures (e.g., Vicuna, Qwen2) and generalize to multiple attention paradigms (e.g., multi-head attention, grouped query attention).
The identified visual heads, discovered using the OCR task, are task-agnostic and remain dominant in other vision-language tasks like object recognition and scene understanding.

key_contributions: The first systematic investigation into how visual concepts are processed within LLMs.
Discovery and formalization of the 'sparsity' and 'universality' properties of visual heads.
A training-free framework for identifying visual heads using OCR-based cross-modal analysis.
Introduction of SparseMM, a practical KV-Cache optimization framework that leverages visual head sparsity for faster inference.
